Abstract—The integration of artificial intel-
ligence (AI) in healthcare is revolutionizing di-
agnosis and patient care by improving clinical
documentation and the management of electronic
health records that depend on medical image
interpretation, increasing accuracy, and reducing
time. Egypt ranks first in liver disease and sec-
ond in liver cancer mortality worldwide in 2020.
Large language models, a subset of AI techniques,
can assist in disease diagnosis. LLM models with
multimodal capabilities can classify and describe
patient scan images and extract information from
clinical notes. These models can extract vital di-
agnoses with the support of prompt engineering,
as one of these models can answer questions,
summarize information, and translate complex
medical terminology into plain language, enabling
patients to understand their medical reports and
diagnoses. There are two primary approaches to
achieving this. First, fine-tuning can adapt the
model to medical data, which can be resource-
intensive. The second approach, pre-trained LLM
models can be utilized to leverage pre-trained
modelstoperformthenecessarytasks,focusingon
effectively using prompts to guide the model for
preciseandrelevantoutputs.Thisstudyhighlights
the role of generative AI models by focusing on
prompt engineering, and how carefully crafting
prompts can enhance the effectiveness of LLM
modelsinmedicalapplicationswithhighaccuracy.
It demonstrates this through experiments using
pre-trained models based on semantic similarity
with GPT-4o and BioGPT. Implementing a zero-
shot model for liver tumor classification is one
of the prompt engineering techniques. The per-
formance metrics achieved were impressive, accu-
racy,precision,recall,andF1-scoresare88,81,88,
and 83 percent, respectively.
Index Terms —Generative AI, Prompt Engi-
neering, Large Language Model (LLM), Chat-
GPT, Artificial Intelligence, Medical radiology
reports
I. Introduction
Generative AI can be implemented in prompt
engineering as a cutting-edge approach in natural
language processing (NLP) tasks by improving LLM
performance [1], [2]. The healthcare sector has re-
cently received increasing attention due to its close
relationship with each individual. In generative AI
models, prompt engineering is user-provided textual
input that directs the model’s output, simple queries
to a complex problem. Prompt engineering is a
computer program that can simulate conversations
with human users. The generative AI applications in
healthcare and medicine will grow in significance as
they develop and become more precisely adapted to
the particular conditions and demands of the medical
domain. This is especially helpful when training data
is lacking or certain jobs are too specialized for
broad models, such as [3]–[6]. Factors influencing
prompt criteria are user intent, model understanding,
domain specificity, clarity, specificity, and constraints
[7]. In artificial intelligence (AI), natural language
processing (NLP) is a subfield that can transform
unstructured clinical notes into structured data. It
can also be used to identify medical conditions, med-
ications, and names of lab tests through tasks like
named entity recognition (NER). GPT models are a
notable innovation in NLP that was first introduced
by OpenAI [3].
Large transformers, known as LLMs are com-
posed of either an encoder, a decoder, or both and
are trained in unsupervised learning on a large corpus
of text. One of the earliest examples of this type of
transformer is BERT, an encoder transformer trained
in unsupervised learning to predict masked tokens.
Next-token prediction was utilized by decoder-style
transformers such as GPT. These models have sig-
nificantly advanced various downstream tasks, in-

cluding question answering, code completion, and
summarizing. Several improvements in this model led
to models like GPT-3, GPT-3.5, and most recently
GPT-4 (OpenAI 2023) [8]. Prompt engineering has
demonstrated significant superiority across multiple
areas and has gained major importance in healthcare
because of recent improvements in LLM models.
LLMs can connect radiation oncology data to clinical
applications. Currently, oncologists often have to
spend considerable time reviewing extensive clinical
notes, which include a variety of text documents such
as demographics, operation notes, pathology and
radiology reports, lab results, discharge, and consult
notes stored in the Electronic Medical Record (EMR)
systems [9]. LLM models like ChatGPT can continue
evolving and improving medical applications, such
as radiology reports, adding value and contributing
to human health. Despite this, LLM models have
many limitations, including transient state, proba-
bilistic nature, outdated information, content fabri-
cation, resource intensity, and domain specificity. So,
prompt engineering techniques enhance LLM utility
and mitigate inherent constraints. Prompt engineer-
ing has more powerful tools like a chain of thought
prompting, encouraging the model to be factual
through other means, explicitly ending the prompt
instructions, using the AI to correct itself, generat-
ing different opinions, teaching an algorithm in the
prompt, and affordances [10]. Prompt engineering
optimizes the interaction with the chatbot for exact
findings. Prompt engineering is divided into manual
prompts (zero-shot, one-shot, few-shot, embedding-
based retrieval mechanisms) and automated prompts
(discrete, continuous). Manual prompts depend on
human-crafted prompts. A prompt that combines
instructions, questions, input data, and examples [1],
[7], [10]. Automated prompts are generated using
algorithms or automating methods that can save
time and cost which collect task-specific information.
Prompt engineering has many applications, including
classification, generation, detection, augmentation,
question answering, inference, Zero-shot learning,
and embedding-based retrieval mechanisms. Prompt
engineering can help simplify radiological reports,
extract information from oncology notes, and classify
fractures using free-text reports even with a limited
dataset [5], [11]–[13]. Also, image segmentation forms
the basis of various clinical tasks, including surgical
planning, diagnostic assistance, and surgical naviga-
tion. Segmentation can be classified as Atlas-based,
learning-based methods, and vision foundation mod-
els [14]. This paper delves into the development
and methodology of innovative AI tools, aiming to
address the challenges posed by explicit content in
radiology reports. The integration of GPT-4, a state-
of-the-art generative language model, plays a pivotal
role in categorizing liver tumors. Section 2 demon-
strates related work highlighting the effectiveness of
prompt engineering across various tasks, showcasing
its potential to enhance large language model (LLM)
performance in medical contexts by building on AI
techniques. Next, section 3 shows the methodology of
prompt engineering for this work. After that, section
4 shows the experimental results and evaluation of
the proposed prompt engineering. Finally, section 5
provides a conclusion and future work of the paper.
II. Related Work
Deep learning have been applied in varieties of
application domains [15]–[18]. Among those domain,
Medical doamin [19]–[22]. Particularly, several re-
search efforts used LLM for medical domains. For
example, the authors in [23] discussed Llama 3. The
model is a dense transformer with 405B parameters
and a context window of up to 128K tokens. Llama
3 integrates image, video, and speech capabilities
with a compositional approach. Another research
study [12] used clinical radiology cases to concate-
nate prompting strategies with LLM ChatGPT and
GPT4. Also, this paper [24] used GPT-4, GPT-
3.5-turbo, Gemma-7B and Llama3-8B. The authors
employed prompt engineering to boost accuracy.
GPT-4 achieved the highest accuracy in detecting
disease status. The work in [11] explains that the
study introduces a prompt construction module for
medical language templates that enables extract of
contextual information from electronic health patient
records and generates complete cell embedding. The
authors in [25] demonstrated that AI models can
accurately segment liver tumors without manual an-
notation by using synthetic tumors in CT scans. The
work in [14] concluded with the following contribu-
tions. Firstly, the introduction of the SAM prompt
engineering framework. Secondly, the development of
two efficient image registration strategies that align
images or prompts with the referenced dataset from
the Royal National Orthopaedic Hospital. Third, the
demonstration of improved performance compared to
alternative approaches which require full segmenta-
tion of the reference set and using SAM without the
proposed registration strategy, for clinical image seg-
mentation. So, the prompt engineering approach for
segmentation foundation models depends on match-
ing the input image or prompt to a small dataset of
weakly labeled samples. This alignment allows the
construction of effective prompts for segmentation
foundation models to obtain accurate segmentation,
even for out-of-distribution samples, using as few

A. Problem Identification and Motivation :
Recognizing the need for radiology-friendly ver-
sions of medical radiology reports, our research ad-
dresses the challenge of categorizing liver tumors in
patient reports accurately.
B. Objectives of the Design :
Develop an innovative tool that utilizes ad-
vanced AI techniques to detect whether the patient
is normal or abnormal systematically and the type of
tumor if present.
C. Design and Development
We use the pre-trained model to perform the
desiredtask.Promptengineeringcanbebrokendown
into five key steps. First, we use an appropriate pre-
trained model such as GPT-4o and BioGPT, suitable
for generating radiology reports. Next, design the
prompts for downstream tasks, which can be tailored
to generate radiology reports; this step is called the
prompt engineering process. The third step involves
designing responses based on the task at hand, al-
lowing the model to produce the desired output. In
the fourth step, we expand the paradigm to improve
results or adaptability methods. Finally, researchers
must design training strategies that enable the model
to learn efficiently and effectively, save time, and
improve accuracy in classifying medical texts or an-
swering questions related to specific cases. Fig. 1
explains the following steps:
1) Medical Reports Loading:load the radiology
reports into memory using a Python library such
as Pandas or Pydicom, depending on the format of
the reports. That establishes a starting point for the
analysis process by importing the raw report data.

IV. Results
In our preliminary evaluation of the AI tools,
we conducted a self-evaluation using a sample of
medical reports. The study focused on the contri-
butions of prompt engineering to healthcare natural
language processing applications, including question-
answering systems, text summarization, machine
translation, and tumor classification. AI techniques
demonstrated the capability to adapt to new scenar-
ios with minimal labeled data [11]. Our experiments
involving prompt selection via semantic similarity
with GPT4 yielded the most favorable outcomes
on the validation split. Prompt selection proved
the most effective approach for medical reports and
GPT4. The performance of prompt selection is at-
tributedtothein-contextmedicalreportsselectedfor
semantic similarity with the input dialogue. This ap-
proach generated coherent and accurate summaries.
A. Experiment 1 (Zero-Shot classification)
Fig. 2: The integration of AI technology into tradi-

To classify liver conditions from radiology re-
ports containing CT or MRI scans and unstructured
doctor notes, we utilized a zero-shot classification
model using LLM specifically the ’Facebook/bart-
large-mnli’ model from the Hugging Face transform-
ers library. We crafted specific prompts based on
extracting the medical report content, focusing on
key clinical indicators and findings. Fig. 2 present a
comparative analysis between radiological diagnoses
and AI/ML-based classification outcomes with a high
accuracy score in diagnosing liver tumors. The left
column shows different medical cases, each accompa-
nied by an image from the diagnostic imaging in CT
scans or MRIs with a brief description of the results
from the radiology report. These results demonstrate
how closely the AI model’s predictions align with the
expert radiologist’s diagnosis, providing a quantita-
tive measure of the model’s accuracy and reliability
in interpreting radiological scans. The figure effec-
tively highlights both the potential and limitations
of AI in detecting diagnostic processes, providing
insights into areas where prompt engineering models
perform well and where further refinement might
be needed to match human expert accuracy. The
multi-label=True option allows the model to classify
the text under multiple categories simultaneously,
essential in medical contexts where a patient may

We utilized the BioGPT model, a pre-trained
language model specifically designed for biomedical
text generation, to produce clinically coherent nar-
ratives based on patient-doctor notes. The model
effectively synthesizes detailed medical information,
such as diagnoses and treatment plans. Parameters

of integrating prompt engineering with LLMs for
medical applications. In the concluding section, we
discuss the implications of our findings and potential
directions for future research.
V. Conclusion and future work
The evaluation of the AI tool for processing
medical reports has demonstrated its potential to en-
hance clinical documentation through accurate infor-
mation extraction, meaningful categorization, and ef-
fective visualization. While the tool shows promise in
improving efficiency and supporting clinical decision-
making, further refinement is necessary to address
challenges related to consistency, handling complex
cases, and seamless integration with existing health-
care systems. The use of AI, particularly through
prompt engineering, could potentially reduce the
workload of clinicians by saving time in review-
ing and interpreting reports. However, these savings
may vary depending on the complexity of cases and
specific workflows in different healthcare settings.
Continued development and iterative testing will be
essential to fully realizing its benefits in enhanc-
ing patient care and advancing medical research.
In the future, we can evaluate, debug, and moni-
tor chains built by the LLM framework using the
Lang Smith application before production [30]. Also,
we anticipate significant advancements in Retrieval-
Augmented Generation (RAG), AI agents, and Col-
laborative Robot Workstations (CREW). These de-
velopments are expected to enhance the capabilities

Abstract—The integration of medicine and artificial 
intelligence is creating novel opportunities to gather, analyze, 
and generate groundbreaking medical insights from the vast 
expanse of medical literature. Although these advancements 
hold great promise, difficulties including manual annotation 
and precise data retrieval, and transparency persist. Advanced 
natural language processing (NLP) techniques and large 
language models (LLMs) have shown potential in addressing 
these issues. This paper introduces the Medical K nowledge 
Graph (MedKG), a comprehensive and multidisciplinary 
medical knowledge graph. By utilizing advanced NLP 
algorithms, MedKG extracts millions of entities and converts 
them into triples from a corpus of superior  medical research 
papers published over  the last decade. It categorizes 
unstructured medical information into nine distinct tags, 
including disease names, treatments, diagnoses, symptoms, 
genetics, cellular structures of diseases, patient data, medical 
procedures, and treatment applications, while seamlessly 
integrating the digital object identifier (DOI) of each paper. As 
the latest structured database of medical knowledge, MedKG 
serves as a powerful tool for accelerating medical research and 
lays the groundwork for constructing a more extensive medical 
knowledge graph using full-text medical articles. Moreover, our 
research establishes a foundation for practical knowledge 
management systems based on text mining, applicable not only 
to complex medical systems but also to other professional 
domains.   
Keywords—Medical e ngineering, NLP, Large language model, 
Knowledge graph, Database  
I. INTRODUCTION
Mo
dern medical research has made significant strides, yet 
the evolution of medical treatments often depends on 
traditional, labor-intensive trial-and-error processes combined 
with clinical expertise. These outdated methods slow down 
the advancement of cutting-edge medical therapies. The vast 
and specialized nature of medical science means that 
researchers in one field may find it challenging to access and 
interpret knowledge from other areas. For instance, those 
studying cancer treatments might not fully understand 
findings related to infectious diseases or neurological 
conditions
[1]. Nevertheless, the biological mechanisms 
underlying various diseases are interrelated, and insights from 
one field can inform another. There is a pressing need to 
integrate insights from multiple medical disciplines to speed 
up research progress. However, essential medical knowledge 
is scattered across millions of scientific publications, 
encompassing topics like disease causes, diagnostic methods, 
treatment strategies, and the study of physiological, 
biochemical, and genetic factors, as well as their applications 
in pharmaceuticals, medical devices, and public health. This 
dispersion of knowledge creates obstacles to cross -
disciplinary collaboration and innovation
[2]. A major shortfall 
in the current medical research framework is the lack of a 
comprehensive database that can bring together this dispersed 
information, making it more accessible and promoting 
interdisciplinary work. 
Even though databases like PubMed, Web of Science, and 
the Cochrane Library provide means to search for medical 
research papers using specific labels, extracting valuable 
medical information from the extensive body of literature 
continues to be a formidable challenge . To gain a clearer 
understanding of medical conditions and treatments, 
structured databases such as ClinVar, OMIM, and DrugBank 
have been developed
[3]. However, these databases primarily 
contain data from clinical trials and genetic studies, which 
may not always fully align with real-world patient outcomes. 
Thus, the medical field urgently requires a database that 
leverages real patient data and clinical practice
[4]. Furthermore, 
medical information in  medicine is not limited to disease 
diagnosis and treatment options, but also includes patient 
demographics, comorbidities, and treatment outcomes. For 
example, patients with chronic conditions often require a 
personalized treatment plan based on their specific needs, 
medical history
[5], and genetic characteristics. In this context, 
specific databases focused on patient outcomes and treatment 
effects have the potential to provide more valuable 
information to medical professionals and researchers than 
general medical databases such as PubMed that may not

capture the nuances of personalized medicine [6]. This 
highlights the necessity of creating specialized databases to 
address the specific requirements of medical research and

Knowledge graphs (KGs) are pivotal in the medical 
domain, offering structured representations of medical 
information
[7], including controlled vocabulary and 
ontological relations. These graphs, comprised of nodes and 
edges, enable complex queries and insights crucial for medical 
research and decision -making. Despite their potential, 
constructing KGs in the medical field demands extensive 
input from domain experts due to the intricacies and 
specialized n ature of medical knowledge
[8]. This labor -
intensive process hampers scalability, impacts performance, 
and poses challenges in maintaining timely and accurate data. 
The swift progress in natural language processing (NLP) has 
transformed the extraction of information from unstructured 
medical text, enabling the efficient and precise creation of 
medical knowledge graphs
[9]. For example, the development 
of Medical Conditions Knowledge Graph (MCKG) has 
showcased the capability to integrate medical information 
from diverse sources, offering a unified view of medical 
knowledge, including disease etiology, diagnostic methods, 
treatment options, and genetic aspects. Specialized medical 
knowledge graphs such as Cardiovascular Disease 
Knowledge Graph (CVD -KG) and Neuro logical Disorders 
Knowledge Graph (ND-KG) have also emerged, focusing on 
specific medical areas and aiding in potential treatment 
discoveries.  
However, constructing and maintaining medical 
knowledge graphs face significant challenges. Despite 
reduced dependency on experts with advancements in NLP, 
extensive annotation of training data remains essential to 
ensure accuracy and reliability
[10]. Furthermore, predicting 
relationships between medical entities to form triples in the 
KG can sometimes lack authenticity, affecting the credibility 
of the graph. Updating medical knowledge graphs is another 
hurdle, as new medical entities require accurate relationship 
predictions with existing entities, complicating the graph's 
dynamic and accurate maintenance. To tackle these issues, the 
appearance of large language models (LLMs) like 
MedicalBERT and ClinicalBERT presents promising 
solutions. These LLMs, fine -tuned for medical text task, 
significantly enhance the extraction and credibility of 
structured medical informat ion. We adopt such LLMs to 
improve tasks like Named Entity Recognition (NER) and 
Relation Extraction (RE) without extensive manual efforts, 
thus advancing medical knowledge graph construction and 
utilization in medical research and decision support systems. 
In this research, we have made significant strides in 
developing a Medical Knowledge Graph (MedKG), an 
advanced graph database tailored specifically for medical 
research and knowledge organization. Our contributions are 
prominently featured in three main domains: (1) We present a 
novel approach for attaining exceptional precision in named 
entity recognition (NER), relation extraction (RE), and entity 
resolution (ER) within medical texts. This method efficiently 
converts unstructured medical data into triples while 
preserving the source information of each triplet, enabling 
convenient updates to the MedKG.  (2) We constructed a 
medical knowledge graph (MedKG) containing partial 
medical information. MedKG provides healthcare 
professionals and medical researchers with access to 
comprehensive medical knowledge through inquiry, 
facilitating in -depth exploration and analysis. (3) We 
implemented a well- defined labeling system in MedKG to 
enhance scalability and facilitate potential integration with 
other disciplines' structured databases or knowledge graphs.

ETHODS 
A. Experimental principle 
The comprehensive workflow of our medica l research is 
shown in Figure 1. We utilize Named Entity Recognition 
(NER) and Relation Extraction (RE) tasks to derive structured 
information about disease information, treatments, and drug 
composition. Subsequently, entity resolution (ER) and 
standardization processes are applied to integrate information 
from these domains into a cohesive medical knowledge graph. 
 
Figure 1. Workflow chart 
The workflow outlined in Figure 2 begins with manual 
annotation and normalization of an initial training dataset, 
followed by fine -tuning of a large language model (LLM) 
specifically tailored for NER and RE tasks related to 
pharmaceutical entities. The resulting inference dataset is 
partitioned into ten segments, an essential phase for the 
following iterative procedure. Next, ER tasks are performed 
using natural language processing (NLP) techniques such as 
ChemDataExtractor, mat2vec, and in-house developed expert 
dictionaries. Following ER, superior outcomes are thoroughly 
curated to expand the training dataset , th us improving the 
model’s effectiveness in future versions. 
 
Figure 2. Pipeline of the fine-tuned LLM for knowledge graph tasks 
Ultimately, the medical knowledge graph is formed using 
the extracted trios from the normalized results obtained after 
the final iteration. This systematic approach ensures the 
integration of disparate medical information into a structured 
and accessible k nowledge graph, thereby facilitating 
comprehensive analysis and research in the medical field.

B. Data preparation and schema design 
Medical experts carefully annotated 9 different categories 
from 55 medical research paper abstracts as a training dataset 
for a large language model (LLM). These categories include 
basic tags such as “disease name,” “treatment,” or 
“prescription,” which, although in different formats, always 
represent specific medical concepts in different papers. 
Additionally, supplemental label s include "Symptom 
Description," "Biological Structure/Function," "Clinical 
Application," "Patient Outcomes," "Treatment Methods," and 
"Diagnostic Methods." The "Patient Outcomes" tab 
summarizes quantitative results such as "Survival," "Recovery 
Time," and "Mortality," while the "Symptom Description" tab 
contains qualitative descriptions such as "Acute," "Severe," 
and "Severe." Taking into account the practical aspects of 
medical applications, we have also annotated under the 
"Patient Outcomes" tab essential parameters that are directly 
relevant to a specific "Clinical Application". For example, 
attributes such as the “efficacy rate” of vaccines, the 
“operative success rate” of surgeries, and the “duration of pain 
relief” of drugs are systematically annotated. And the central 
node "Medical Entity" is crucial, connected to nodes 
describing its nomenclature, composition and various 
properties. Specifically, "medical entity" includes "medical 
name", "disease name" and "abbreviation". “Biological 
structure/function” describes its anatomical or physiological 
aspects; “clinical application” refers to its actual medical use; 
and “patient outcomes” outlines its impact on patient health. 
The "Medical Entity" node also links to the "Symptom 
Description" node which provi des additional qualitative 
information. In addition, the "Clinical Application" node 
branches into "Patient Outcomes," "Symptom Descriptions," 
and "Medical Domains," Suggesting additional specifications 
and contextual relevance for medical applications. To identify 
the origins of nodes and relationships, each node is associated 
with a Digital Object Identifier (DOI) node. By examining the 
shared “DOI” node neighbors at both ends of a relationship, 
we can pinpoint the source article from which the relationship 
originated. 
Considering the complexity of medical sentences and the 
variety of terms used in abstracts, a normalization process was 
applied following the initial extraction. This standardization is 
intended to provide a consistent representation of entities with 
similar meanings in medical texts. For instance, terms like  
“cardiovascular disease” and “heart disease” were 
standardized to “cardiovascular disease,” while terms such as 
“surgical intervention approach,” “postoperative care 
approach,” and “therapy-based approach” were simplified to 
“treatment procedure.” ". Undergoing this normalization 
process, all annotated entities achieve consistency, which in 
turn facilitates the efficient training of language models 
(LLMs). We have collected 100,000 abstra cts of medically 
relevant peer -reviewed research articles from authoritative 
medical databases, each abstract is organized in JSON format 
with a "DOI - text" structure to facilitate smooth processing 
and thorough analysis. 
C. LLMs training, evaluation and inference 
The training dataset includes aggregated information used 
for fine-tuning models such as BioMedLM, LLama2 7B, and 
ChatGLM 6B. After achieving high -quality outcomes from 
normalized inference, we repeatedly retrained the fine-tuned 
LLM to predict following data sets. The models were trained 
for 8 iterations with a single-sample batch size. Furthermore, 
we labeled 50 abstracts to evaluate the LLM’s performance. 
Our assessment  focuses on the model's Named Entity 
Recognition (NER) capabilities and explores its ability to 
identify potential internal relationships among sets of related 
elements in the Relationship Extraction (RE) task. 
Furthermore, during the data compilation phase, we 
standardized entities and evaluated the effectiveness of the 
LLMs in this standardization process. For tasks such as 
Named Entity Recognition (NER), Relation Extraction (RE), 
and Entity Resolution (ER), our performance evaluation 
utilized a comprehensive framework, focusing on metrics 
such as accuracy, sensitivity, and harm onic mean, while 
considering errors like incorrect positives and missed 
detections.

In the domain of medical text analysis, a true positive (TP) 
signifies the accurate identification of an entity, while in 
Relationship Extraction (RE), it indicates the correct 
identification of a relationship between medical entities. In 
Entity Resolution (ER), An accurate positive denotes the exact 
alignment of an entity with the given schema. On the other 
hand, a false positive (FP) happens when the model 
incorrectly detects an entity, relationship, or standardization, 
while an overlooked positive (FN) s ignifies the model’s 
failure to recognize a correct entity, relation, or schema 
element. Based on these definitions, we can assess each task 
using Equations (1), (2), and (3) . After performing the 
evaluation, we select a fine- tuned Large Language Model 
(LLM) that shows optimal performance in NER and RE tasks 
to process 100,000 abstracts iteratively. The results of these 
inferences are formatted as “DOI - text - response.” Thus, the 
fine-tuned LLM performs both entity recognition and 
categorization, achieving NER and RE tasks simultaneously. 
Additionally, all identified entities and relationships in the 
output are trackable, improving data integrity and usability. 
D. Data matching 
The accuracy and reliability of a Medical Knowledge 
Graph (KG) are paramount, necessitating a meticulous review 
and correction of inference results before constructing the 
graph. To standardize core medical labels, we implement a 
two-step process: ER-NF/A to ER-N/F. During the ER-NF/A 
phase, we utilize specialized medical extraction too ls to 
extract medical terms and their corresponding acronyms from 
abstracts. Subsequently, we employ the MedNLP  model for 
utilizing word embeddings to compare entities identified by

For normalizing objects and connections  in other labels

Specifically, we create a method for dynamic vector clustering

without predefined cluster numbers. This adaptive clustering 
method employs a threshold of similarity to establish group 
memberships, allowing medical entities to join or form

In the domain of medical literature analysis, we employed 
domain experts to categorize clusters, yielding approximately

as anatomical structures, physiological phases, clinical

patient outcomes, and disease descriptions. Subsequently, we 
applied analytical methodologies to integrate this expert -

Following a thorough similarity comparison process, we

of "Characteristics" and "Attributes" labels often extends 
beyond specific medical domains and may include a wide

To enhance the quality of our training dataset, we

normalized inference results of each iterative process,

Data is characterized by its precision and recall measurements,

we conducted a rigorous evaluation of the fine-tuned natural 
language model, trained on progressively refined datasets, to 
ensure optimal performance in analyzing and interpreting

E. Knowledge graph construction 
To organize the deduced outcomes into triples in the 
medical domain, we utilize three labels that denote the 
medical concept as the primary tag, where it serves as the main 
component, the names of other labels constitute the 
relationships, and their values represent the associated details. 
Within the structure of primary labels, "Symptom" is given the 
highest priority, followed by "Disease", and then "Treatment". 
Additionally, each main concept and its associated details are 
linked to the Digital Object Identifier (DOI) of the source 
article from which the information was extracted. This linkage 
enables us to trace the origin of the relationship between any 
two concepts by examining the overlap in their associated 
entities. Subsequently, we convert the triples into a Medical 
Knowledge Graph (MedKG) and store it using the Neo4j 
graph database, which also supports subgraph matching 
functionality. This feature aligns well with the requirement to 
search for specific medical concepts based on user -input 
criteria. To enhance accessibility to the comprehensive data, 
we also offer the data set available in  CSV format for 
convenient information management.

IV. CONCLUSION 
In this research, we introduce a novel natural language 
processing (NLP) pipeline tailored for constructing 
knowledge graphs (KGs) aimed at efficiently extracting 
triplets extracted from unstructured scientific text. The unique 
aspect of this method lies in its capacity to fine -tune a large 
language model (LLM) with minimal data annotation. This 
enables the fine-tuned LLM to extract structured information 
from vast amounts of unstructured text. By bypassing 
predictive algorithms, this approach ensures the highest level 
of authenticity and traceability for the structured information. 
Utilizing this technique, we created an operational medicine 
knowledge graph (MedKG) containing medical material and 
related knowledge sourced from abstracts of 100,000 peer -
reviewed papers. Subsequent analysis validated the 
effectiveness and credibility of MedKG. 
Our methodology and the resulting knowledge graph 
possess considerable potential across multiple dimensions. 
Firstly, by deepening the extraction of structured data, 
including entire research articles, we aim to create a more 
thorough and comprehensive knowledge graph. This entails 
not only broadening the range of data  analysis but also 
enhancing methods to encapsulate  subtleties in intricate 
academic literature. Furthermore, improving identifiers for 
entities in our framework facilitates finer data classification, 
including the addition of granular attributes like preparation 
parameters or precise parameters . This enhancement 
substantially increases the level of detail and usefulness of the 
knowledge graph. In addition, the flexibility of our NLP 
process demonstrates its adaptability across various scientific 
fields, providing a foundation for building specialized 
knowledge networks. Finally, integrating MedKG integrating 
with existing knowledge graphs enables the creation of 
interconnected and holistic  datasets, fostering cutting -edge 
research and application development in medical science and 
other fields.

Abstract— Pulmonary function tests (PFTs) are vital for 
diagnosing various pulmonary conditions, including chronic 
obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and asthma. Traditional 
PFTs, conducted using laboratory -based spirometers, are 
accurate but costly and require s killed technicians. Recent 
advancements in portable spirometry and large language models 
(LLMs) offer promising alternatives for remote diagnostics and 
clinical decision support. This study evaluates the performance of 
three advanced LLMs: Gemini 1.5 Pro, GPT 4o, and Claude 3.5 
Sonnet in understanding and interpreting PFTs data. The models 
were assessed using three prompt types: zero shot, guidelines 
enhanced, and few shot, and their performance was measured in 
terms of accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and processing speed. 
Results indicate that Claude 3.5 Sonnet consistently outperformed 
the other models across all metrics, demonstrating superior 
comprehension and classification abilities. Error analysis revealed 
specific areas for improvemen t, particularly in logical reasoning 
and adherence to guidelines. The findings highlight the potential 
of LLMs to enhance diagnostic processes and reduce healthcare 
costs, while also emphasizing the need for further research to 
address data privacy, intero perability, and ethical considerations 
for clinical integration. Future efforts should focus on leveraging 
open-source models and expanding datasets to optimize LLMs for 
real-world medical applications.

I. INTRODUCTION 
Pulmonary function tests (PFTs) are essential diagnostic 
tools used to assess lung health and diagnose various pulmonary 
conditions, including chronic obstructive pulmonary disease 
(COPD), asthma, and restrictive lung diseases [1-3]. The Global 
Initiative for Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease (GOLD) report 
highlights that COPD is a leading cause of morbidity and 
mortality worldwide, necessitating accurate diagnostic methods 
[4]. Traditionally, PFTs are conducted using laboratory -based 
spirometers, which prov ide precise measurements but come 
with high costs and require skilled technicians. This makes the 
approach impractical and leads to substantial increases in 
medical expenses for patients directed to tertiary hospitals [5]. 
Several studies have indicated that measurements obtained 
using portable spirometers are nearly identical to those from 
traditional, laboratory-based spirometers [6, 7]. This suggests 
that portable spirometers, with their cost -effectiveness, 
portability, and ease of use, can serve as a viable alternative to 
conventional PFTs devices [8]. Recently, the integration of 
portable spirometry with mobile applications has enabled 
patients to easily measure their lung capacity at home and 
transmit the results to hea lthcare providers. This advancement 
goes beyond the development of remote treatment plans, 
extending to the creation of diverse software services related to 
pulmonary health [9-11].  
Moreover, with the advent of large language models (LLMs), 
the medical field has seen substantial advancements [12], 
including the development of medical domain LLMs capable of 
passing medical licensing exams [13], as well as applications in 
disease predic tion and diagnostic consultation chatbots [14]. 
Ongoing research is also aimed at creating LLMs that can 
perform tasks beyond simple algorithmic classification of 
pulmonary function test data, generating pulmonary function 
test charts at the proficiency le vel of medical residents [15]. 
Such innovations have the potential to significantly reduce 
unnecessary medical expenditures and alleviate the burden of 
frequent hospital visits for patients.

by the NRF grant funded by the Korean government (MSIT) (No. RS-2024-00457381).

Therefore, the aim of this study is to conduct foundational 
research by evaluating and comparing the comprehension of 
pulmonary function test data among three leading state -of-the-
art large parameter LLMs, namely the Gemini 1.5 Pro [16], the 
GPT 4o [17] an d the Claude 3.5 Sonnet [18], which have 
demonstrated top tier performance across various assessments. 
II. METHODOLOGY 
The methodology section provides a comprehensive 
overview of the experimental design, data collection, 
preprocessing, and the specific approaches used to evaluate the 
performance of three advanced LLMs in interpreting PFTs data. 
A. Data Collection and Preprocessing 
The study involved a total of 200 participants who were 
recruited from various primary healthcare settings. Participants 
included individuals across different age groups, genders, and 
varying health statuses to ensure a representative sample. Each 
participant provided informed consent before participating in the 
study, ensuring ethical standards were maintained. 
Data were collected using the portable spirometer BULO M, 
developed by BREATHINGS Co., Ltd. This device is known for 
its accuracy and portability, which makes it ideal for use in both 
clinical and home settings. The spirometer measured two key 
pulmonary parameters: forced vital capacity (FVC) and forced 
expiratory volume in one second (FEV1). These parameters are 
critical in diagnosis of pulmonary diseases. 
The predicted normal values for FVC and FEV1, used to 
classify pulmonary disease types, were calculated using Dr. 
Choi's equation [19], a widely accepted formula for predicting 
normal values based on the Asian population and considering 
age, sex, height, and weight. 
This normalization was essential to account for individual 
variations and ensure that the comparisons made across 
participants were valid. 
Using the predicted normal values for FVC and FEV1, the 
data were classified into four pulmonary disease categories: 
normal, restrictive, obstructive, or combined. The classification 
followed established diagnostic guidelines: 
• Normal: FEV1/FVC ratio of 70% or higher and FVC 80% 
or higher of the predicted normal value for FVC; labeled 
as ‘normal’. 
• Restrictive: FEV1/FVC ratio of 70% or higher and FVC 
less than 80% of the predicted normal value for FVC; 
labeled as ‘restrictive’. 
• Obstructive: FEV1/FVC ratio of less than 70% and FVC 
80% or higher of the predicted normal value for FVC; 
labeled as ‘obstructive’. 
• Combined: Meeting the criteria for both restrictive and 
obstructive conditions; labeled as ‘combined’. 
The ground truth labels for each participant were determined 
based on the above contents. 
To facilitate the evaluation of the LLMs, the numerical PFTs 
data were transformed into standardized sentences. These 
sentences followed a specific structure, as outlined in the study's 
guidelines, to ensure consistency and clarity. Each sentence 
included the measured FVC and FEV1 values and the predicted 
normal values for these measurements to maintain uniformity. 
B. Large Language Models 
The study evaluated three state of the art LLMs: Gemini 1.5 
Pro (developed by Google), GPT 4o (developed by OpenAI), 
and Claude 3.5 Sonnet (developed by Anthropic). These models 
were chosen based on their superior performance across various 
natural languag e processing tasks and their availability via 
application programming interfaces. 
Each model was accessed through its respective application 
programming interface and configured with specific parameters 
to ensure consistent performance across all tests: 
• Temperature: Set to 0.0 to ensure deterministic outputs. 
This parameter controls the randomness of the model's 
predictions, with a lower temperature resulting in more 
consistent and predictable responses. 
• Top p: Set to 1.0, which means the entire probability 
distribution of possible tokens was considered during the 
generation of responses. This setting ensures that no 
potential answers are excluded from consideration. 
C. Prompts for Pulmonary Disease Type Classification 
Prompt engineering is a crucial aspect of evaluating LLMs, 
as the quality and structure of prompts can significantly impact 
the models’ performances. This study employed three types of 
prompts to assess the models’ abilities to interpret PFTs data and 
generate accurate clinical rationales. 
Each case was processed in a separate session to ensure that 
prior inputs did not influence subsequent responses, maintaining 
the integrity and independence of each test case. 
Zero shot prompting involves providing the model with 
PFTs data without any additional context or examples. This type 
of prompt assesses the model's inherent ability to understand and 
interpret the data based solely on its pre -existing knowledge. 
Each model was given a prompt structured as follows, 
corresponding to Fig. 1: 
• Entry of transformed PFTs data. 
• A request to generate a diagnosis (normal, restrictive, 
obstructive, or combined). 
• A request to provide a clinical rationale in four sentences. 
To improve the models' performance, zero shot prompt were 
enhanced with specific diagnostic guidelines and the chain of 
thought (CoT) technique [20]. The guidelines provided explicit 
criteria for classifying pulmonary diseases, while the CoT 
technique involved breaking down the reasoning process into 
sequential steps. The guidelines enhanced prompt structure 
included the following, corresponding to Fig. 1: 
• The same PFTs data as provided in the zero shot prompt. 
• Detailed diagnostic guidelines to assist the model in 
making accurate classifications. 
• CoT sentences to guide the model through the reasoning 
process step by step.

Few shot prompting involves providing the model with a 
small number of example cases in addition to the primary 
prompt. This approach helps the model understand the task 
better by learning from the examples. For this study, four 
example datasets were included in the prompts. The structure 
of the few shot prompt, as shown in Fig. 1, was as follows: 
• The same PFTs data input as the previous two prompts. 
• The same diagnostic guidelines and CoT sentences 
input as the previous prompts. 
• Input of four example responses. 
D. Evaluation for Classification 
The quantitative performance of the LLMs in classifying 
different types of pulmonary diseases was assessed through a 
variety of evaluation metrics: 
• Accuracy: The proportion of correct predictions out of 
the total predictions. 
• Precision: The ratio of true positives to the sum of true 
positives and false positives, indicating the model's 
ability to avoid false positives and its reliability in 
positive predictions. 
• Recall: The ratio of true positives to the sum of true 
positives and false negatives, reflecting the model's 
ability to identify all relevant instances and its 
effectiveness in capturing actual positives. 
• F1 Score: The harmonic mean of precision and recall, 
providing a balanced measure of the model's 
performance, particularly useful when there is an 
uneven class distribution. 
These metrics were selected to ensure a comprehensive 
understanding of how well the models performed in 
distinguishing between the four categories of pulmonary 
conditions. 
E. Error Types 
The clinical rationales generated by the LLMs were 
analyzed for different types of errors, including: 
• Comparison Operation Errors (COE): These errors 
occurred when the model's logical operations 
involving numerical comparisons were flawed, despite 
the calculations being correct. 
• Guideline Deviations (GD): These errors happened 
when the model deviated from the provided diagnostic

guidelines, either by offering alternative explanations 
or additional clinical opinions that were not aligned 
with the guidelines. 
• Misdiagnoses (MD): In these cases, the model 
generated a clinical rationale that aligned with the 
diagnostic guidelines, but the final diagnosis was 
incorrect. 
• Miscalculations (MC): These errors involved incorrect 
calculations during the reasoning process, which could 
lead to incorrect conclusions. 
III. RESULTS 
In this section, we present the detailed results of our 
evaluation of the three LLMs: Gemini 1.5 Pro, GPT 4o, and 
Claude 3.5 Sonnet, on their comprehension of PFTs data. The 
evaluation was conducted across three types of prompts: zero 
shot, guidelines enha nced, and few shot. This includes the 
quantitative performance metrics, such as accuracy, precision, 
recall, and F1 score, along with the time efficiency and the 
number of tokens generated by the three models for 200 
iterations to assess their processing speed and efficiency under 
different prompt types. Additionally, we present a 
comprehensive error analysis to provide insights into the 
models' capabilities and limitations.

Pulmonary Disease Type Classification by Prompt 
The zero shot performance of the models was evaluated by 
providing them with only the PFTs results without any 
additional context. Table I summarizes the accuracy, precision, 
recall, and F1 score for each model across the four pulmonary 
disease types: normal, restrictive, obstructive, and combined. 
Claude 3.5 Sonnet outperformed the other models in the 
zero shot setting across all disease types, achieving the highest 
accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score. This indicates that 
Claude 3.5 Sonnet has a superior inherent ability to understand 
and classify pulmonary diseases based solely on the provided 
PFTs data.

C. Error Rate 
In this study, we conducted a detailed error analysis to 
understand the types and frequencies of errors made by 
Gemini 1.5 Pro, GPT 4o, and Claude 3.5 Sonnet in 
interpreting PFTs data. 
Fig. 2 illustrates the error rate for each model across the 
different prompt types (zero shot, guidelines enhanced, and 
few shot). The results reveal that Claude 3.5 Sonnet 
consistently outperformed both Gemini 1.5 Pro and GPT 4o 
in all prompt settings, achieving the lowest error rate. 
In the zero shot setting, Claude 3.5 Sonnet achieved an 
error rate of 20%, significantly lower than GPT 4o (26%) and 
Gemini 1.5 Pro (45%). This indicates that Claude 3.5 Sonnet 
has a superior inherent ability to understand and classify 
pulmonary disease types based on PFTs data wit hout 
additional context.

The introduction of diagnostic guidelines and the CoT 
technique resulted in improved performance for all models. 
Claude 3.5 Sonnet again demonstrated the lowest error rate at

Despite the addition of  guidelines, the error rate for both

D. Types of Errors in Few Shot Prompt 
Fig. 3 categorizes the examples of errors generated by the 
LLMs into four types. COE referred to instances where the 
calculations were correct, but the logical operation involving 
comparisons with values such as 70 or 80 was flawed. GD 
indicated cases where the LLMs deviated from the provided 
diagnostic guidelines during the second and third prompts, 
providing alternative explanations or additional clinical 
opinions.  
MD occurred when the clinical rationale aligns with the 
diagnostic guidelines, but the "diagnosis:" field contained an 
incorrect answer. Lastly, MC denoted calculation errors, 
which were observed only in the Gemini 1.5 Pro model. These 
errors cooccurred with 3 instances of MD, 6 instances of GD, 
and 10 instances of COE. 
Fig. 4 illustrates the number of errors by type generated by 
each model for the few shot prompt. The Gemini 1.5 Pro 
model displayed a balanced distribution of errors, including 
COE, GD, and MD, as well as calculation mistakes. The GPT 
4o model exhibited relati vely fewer errors overall, most of 
which were of the COE type. The Claude 3.5 Sonnet model 
had the fewest errors overall, with the most common type 
being GD, indicating its tendency to provide additional 
clinical insights.

IV. DISCUSSION 
The results highlight the potential of advanced LLMs, 
particularly the Claude 3.5 Sonnet, in the medical field for 
diagnosing pulmonary diseases. Although Claude 3 Opus is a 
different model from the Claude 3.5 Sonnet and is not 
specialized for the medical domain, it demonstrated 
performance [21] on the PubMedQA dataset [22] nearly 
equivalent to Google's Med-PaLM 2 [23], which is designed 
specifically for medical applications. This suggests that the 
contexts of our experiment and those results are similar. 
The ability of these models to accurately interpret PFTs 
data and generate clinical rationales can significantly enhance 
diagnostic processes [24], reduce healthcare costs [25], and 
improve patient outcomes [26, 27]. Integrating such AI 
technologies into c linical settings can provide healthcare 
professionals with reliable decision support tools [28], leading 
to more efficient and accurate diagnoses [29], and potentially 
advancing to specific tasks such as summarizing risk levels 
based on preoperative pulmonary function test results [30]. 
However, implementing LLMs in clinical settings requires 
careful consideration of data privacy and integration with 
existing systems [31]. Key steps include ensuring data security 
through local deployment and anonymization, as well as 
complying with health care regulations like the Health 
Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) and 
establishing interoperability with electronic health record 
(EHR) systems using a standardized application programming 
interface (API) [32, 33]. Additionally, fine tuning LLMs with 
domain specific data and incorporating human oversight are 
crucial for enhancing accuracy and maintaining clinical 
standards [34]. Therefore, balancing technological 
advancements with ethical and practical considerations will be 
critical for the successful integration of LLMs in healthcare 
[35]. 
Furthermore, for the future development of LLMs capable 
of generating pulmonary function test charts for use in real 
world medical settings, it is essential to utilize open sourced 
LLMs [36]. Related research has demonstrated the application 
of large parameter LLMs, such as GPT 4, to clinical data for 
diagnosing Alzheimer's disease and generating clinical

The key aspect is that large parameter LLMs generate

[38]. This study's significance lies in comparing three state of 
the art models, which currently demonstrate superior 
performance, using PFTs data, and analyzing their errors to 
provide insights into the understanding of pulmonary diseases 
by large parameter LLMs. 
Additionally, exploring and fine tuning other open sourced 
LLMs can help identify models with even greater potential for 
clinical applications [39]. Further efforts should focus on 
minimizing specific types of errors, such as COEs and GDs, 
to enhance the models' reliability and accuracy [40]. 
V. CONCLUSION 
This study evaluated the performance of three state of the 
art LLMs: Gemini 1.5 Pro, GPT 4o, and Claude 3.5 Sonnet in 
interpreting PFTs data and generating clinical rationales for 
diagnosing pulmonary diseases. Notably, there are no recent 
studies that directly compare the performance of these latest 
models on specific clinical tasks, making this research a novel 
contribution to the field. 
The Claude 3.5 Sonnet consistently outperformed the 
Gemini 1.5 Pro and the GPT 4o across all evaluation metrics 
and prompt types. It demonstrated the highest accuracy, 
precision, recall, and F1 score, indicating its superior ability 
to understand and class ify PFTs data. The model's 
performance was enhanced by the inclusion of diagnostic 
guidelines and few shot examples, showcasing its capacity to 
learn effectively from additional context. The Gemini 1.5 Pro 
and the GPT 4o also showed improvements with enhan ced 
prompts, but their overall performance remained below that of 
the Claude 3.5 Sonnet.  
From the perspective of error analysis, the Gemini 1.5 Pro 
exhibited a balanced distribution of errors, including COEs, 
GDs, and MDs, indicating a need for better logical reasoning 
and adherence to guidelines. The GPT 4o, while 
demonstrating fewer errors o verall, struggled primarily with 
COEs, suggesting a need for improved numerical 
interpretation capabilities. 
While this study provides valuable insights, it is limited by 
the sample size and the specific models evaluated. Future 
research should involve larger and more diverse datasets to 
better generalize the findings. Additionally, further efforts 
should focus on minimizing specific types of errors, such as 
COEs and GDs, to enhance the models' reliability and 
accuracy.
